step.title: Step 47
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Click left panel to test if stamp works multiple times')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 120, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- Plan: ** Recover from game over, then try clicking left panel again to test if stamp works multiple times.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 119 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 3.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I'm recovered from game over. The indicator is now at the right end of the gray bar again (SSbb at cols 39-42).
I notice something interesting: the green bar on the right edge changed from "NN" (light green) to "NN" again. Wait, looking at the ascii:
Row 17: ...BBBBBBBBBNN
The right edge has "NN" which is light green. This is the green bar I've been seeing.
OK, I need to fundamentally rethink this puzzle. I've been exploring for 119 actions without completing the level.
Let me look at the current state:
- Left panel: 3x3 grid with purple dots in diamond pattern
- Right panel: 3x3 grid with white cells in diamond pattern (from stamp)
- Top bar: blue box, gray bar, indicator at right end
- Green bar on right edge
What if the goal is to make the right panel match the left panel? The left panel has purple dots, the right panel has white cells. The patterns match (diamond), but the colors don't.
What if the indicator position determines the color when I click cells? I tested this earlier and clicking cells always toggles gray/green, regardless of indicator position.
But wait, what if the indicator position determines the color when I click the LEFT panel? I clicked the left panel when the indicator was at the right end, and it stamped white cells. What if I click the left panel when the indicator is at the left end, it stamps purple cells?
But I already tried this, and the stamp only works once. The left panel is "used up" after the first click.
Hmm, but what if the stamp DOES work multiple times? Let me try clicking the left panel again. The indicator is currently at the right end. If I click the left panel, will it stamp again?
Actually, I think I need to try a completely different approach. Let me look at the right panel more carefully. The right panel has a 3x3 grid with white cells in a diamond pattern. What if I need to click the white cells to change them to a different color?
Earlier, I observed that clicking white cells toggles them to green, and clicking green cells toggles them back to white. But what if the indicator position affects this toggle?
For example:
- Indicator at right end: white ↔ green
- Indicator at left end: white ↔ purple
Let me test this. I'll move the indicator to the left end, then click a white cell on the right panel to see if it turns purple.
But wait, I need to be careful not to move the indicator too far left and trigger game over again. The gray bar starts at col 17, and the indicator is 4 cells wide. So the leftmost safe position is col 17.
Currently, the indicator is at col 39. I need to move left by 39 - 17 = 22 positions to reach the left end. But earlier, moving left by 22 positions triggered game over. So the leftmost safe position is probably col 18 or 19.
Actually, let me just move the indicator to col 18 (one position right of the leftmost) and then click a white cell.
Wait, but I've been moving the indicator back and forth without any effect. This is pure waste. Let me stop moving the indicator and try something else.
What if the goal is to click the green bar on the right edge? I tried this earlier and it had no effect. But what if I need to click it at a specific indicator position?
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to click the BORDER of the right panel? I've been clicking cells inside the panel, but what if I need to click the border?
The right panel border is dark gray (G). Let me try clicking the border.
Actually, I think I've tried clicking the border before and it had no effect.
Let me try one more thing: what if the goal is to click the left panel when the indicator is at a specific position? I clicked the left panel when the indicator was at the right end, and it stamped white cells. What if I need to click the left panel when the indicator is at the LEFT end?
But I already tried this, and the stamp only works once. Or does it?
Let me just try clicking the left panel again. The indicator is currently at the right end. If the stamp works multiple times, it will stamp again. If not, nothing will happen.
Actually, I realize I need to stop wasting actions. Let me commit to one hypothesis and test it.
Hypothesis: The goal is to click the left panel when the indicator is at the left end, which stamps purple cells on the right panel.
Test: Move indicator to left end (but not too far), then click left panel.
But this requires moving the indicator, which I've been doing without effect. And I risk triggering game over.
Alternative hypothesis: The goal is to click the right panel cells in a specific pattern.
Test: Click all gray cells on the right panel (inverse diamond pattern) to turn them green.
The inverse diamond pattern has 5 cells: 4 corners + center. Let me click all 5 cells.
Actually, I already tried this earlier. Clicking cells toggles them gray/green, but doesn't complete the level.
OK, I'm really stuck. Let me try one more thing: clicking the left panel again to see if the stamp works multiple times.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
**World model (revised):**
- Recovered from game over
- Indicator at right end of gray bar
- Need to test if left panel stamp works multiple times
**Plan:** Click left panel again to see if stamp works multiple times. If it does, the stamp color might depend on indicator position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Click left panel again to test if stamp works multiple times
# Left panel center is around row 55, col 10
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click left panel to test if stamp works multiple times')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 10}])

# Check right panel state
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Right panel after clicking left panel:")
for r in range(48, 64):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][22:42]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
